corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

location.sentence: (4) Notice that our model can be viewed as training two “au- toencoders” [20]: we learn one autoencoder F ◦ G : X → X jointly with another G ◦ F : Y → Y .

top_candidates.sentence: Handling more varied and extreme transformations, especially geometric changes, is an important problem for future work.
We adopt the architecture for our generative networks from Johnson et al. who have shown impressive results for neural style transfer and superresolution.
Such a patch-level discriminator architecture has fewer parameters than a full-image discriminator and can work on arbitrarilysized images in a fully convolutional fashion .
The tasks include semantic labels↔photo on the Cityscapes dataset , and map↔aerial photo on data scraped from Google Maps.
Figure shows some example results on other paired datasets used in "pix2pix" , such as architectural labels↔photos from the CMP Facade Database , and edges↔shoes from the UT Zappos50K dataset .

Figure 1: Given any two unordered image collections X and Y , our algorithm learns to automatically "translate" an image from one into the other and vice versa: (left) Monet paintings and landscape photos from Flickr; (center) zebras and horses from ImageNet; (right) summer and winter Yosemite photos from Flickr.
We adopt an adversarial loss to learn the mapping such that the translated images cannot be distinguished from images in the target domain.
Finally, we demonstrate the generality of our algorithm on a wide range of applications where paired data does not exist.
We follow the same perceptual study protocol from Isola et al. , except we only gather data from 25 participants per algorithm we tested.
Participants were shown a sequence of pairs of images, one a real photo or map and one fake (generated by our algorithm or a baseline), and asked to click on the image they thought was real.
The remaining 40 trials were used to assess the rate at which each algorithm fooled participants.
Each session only tested a single algorithm, and participants were only allowed to complete a single session.

From left to right: input, BiGAN/ALI [7, 9], CoGAN , feature loss + GAN, SimGAN , CycleGAN (ours), pix2pix trained on paired data, and ground truth. tributed from those tested in (due to running the experiment at a different date and time).

For example, our method has got confused in the horse → zebra example (Figure , right), because our model was trained on the wild horse and zebra synsets of ImageNet, which does not contain images of a person riding a horse or zebra.

downstream_contract.score_warning: Scores are ranking and filtering signals, not calibrated probabilities.
